coredata.dc:title: Study on construction of red carbon nanodots from O-phenylenediamine
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Yaqing
Bai, Jianliang
Huang, Zhujun
Wu, Shuang
Wang, Xinyu
Yuan, Guojun
Zhang, Lu
Ren, Lili
coredata.dc:description: Red emission carbon nanodots (CNDs) have a promising prospect in bioimaging field because of their low-toxicity, good compatibility, slight photo damage and low interference. However, it is hard to prepare red photoluminescence (PL) CNDs. The CNDs prepared from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) by solvothermal usually emit green fluorescence (FL). The emission wavelength only turned 32 nm red-shift after oxidative polymerization by introducing FeCl3 as catalyst. While, as terephthalic acid (TA) was introduced into the reaction, the red-shift exceeded 100 nm and the red emission was successfully achieved. Thus, according to the special structure of precursors, a donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) structure strategy was proposed to synthesize red emission CNDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon materials
Nanoparticles
Luminescence

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the two routes to prepare Y-CNDs and R-CNDs. TEM image of (b) G-CNDs, (c) Y-CNDs and (d) R-CNDs. Size distribution histogram of (e) G-CNDs, (f) Y-CNDs and (g) R-CNDs.

UV/Vis absorption, emission spectra and inset photographs under day light (left) and UV light (365 nm) (right) of (a) G-CNDs, (b) Y-CNDs and (c) R-CNDs in ethanol solvent. 3D waterfall FL spectra of (d) G-CNDs, (e) Y-CNDs and (f) R-CNDs dispersed in ethanol under different excitation wavelengths. (g)The possible resulted structures of G-CNDs and R-CNDs.

(a) C 1 s, (b) N 1 s and (c) O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of G-CNDs. (d) C 1 s, (e) N 1 s and (f) O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of Y-CNDs. (g) C 1 s, (h) N 1 s and (i) O 1 s high-resolution XPS spectra of R-CNDs.

head.ce:title: Study on construction of red carbon nanodots from O-phenylenediamine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing 211189, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Red emission carbon nanodots (CNDs) have a promising prospect in bioimaging field because of their low-toxicity, good compatibility, slight photo damage and low interference. However, it is hard to prepare red photoluminescence (PL) CNDs. The CNDs prepared from o-phenylenediamine (oPD) by solvothermal usually emit green fluorescence (FL). The emission wavelength only turned 32 nm red-shift after oxidative polymerization by introducing FeCl3 as catalyst. While, as terephthalic acid (TA) was introduced into the reaction, the red-shift exceeded 100 nm and the red emission was successfully achieved. Thus, according to the special structure of precursors, a donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) structure strategy was proposed to synthesize red emission CNDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are a new member of the family of carbon nanostructures [1]. Up to now, CNDs have been widely used in fluorescence sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis and other fields due to their good stability, low toxicity and abundant low-cost sources [2–6]. However, although many of precursors and different synthetic methods have been developed, most of the reported CNDs emitted blue to green photoluminescence (PL) under ultraviolet (UV) light [7–8]. The shortwave emission can cause massive interference from the background emission of biological tissues and the intensity of emission wavelength decreases with the increase of long excitation wavelength normally. All these problems heavily hinder the further applications of CNDs in bioimaging [9–10].

O-phenylenediamine (oPD) is one of the hottest precursors of the preparation of CNDs, which can obtain green PL and named G-CNDs [11]. According to the literatures, researchers used oPD to react with different acids or salts to obtain red emission CNDs, it is commonly believed that PL properties of red emission CNDs are derived from the surface states, molecular states, larger conjugate structure and crosslinked aggregation chromophores [12–16]. However, the existing results and discussions are insufficient to provide a clear and unified explanation for the luminescence mechanism of CNDs. From this foundation, there are two routes to realize the red-shift of the PL. 1) For N-doped CNDs, the content and form of nitrogen-atoms will cause significant changes in surface properties, such as surface defects, surface functional groups and interactions between adjacent atoms, thus lead to shift of the PL. 2) The surface functionalization and passivation of CNDs also can realize the control of multi-color fluorescence. Thus, we propose two kinds of strategies (Fig. 1
a). 1) FeCl3 which is conducive to catalyzed oxidative polymerization will be introduced and obtain Y-CNDs. 2) Terephthalic acid (TA) will be added into the initial reaction to modify the surface of CNDs and obtain R-CNDs.

0.7 g oPD was dissolved into ethyl alcohol and then transferred to a high-pressure autoclave and reacted for 12 h under 180 ℃. 0.1 g oPD and 50 μL 100 mM FeCl3 solution were introduced into deionized water and then reacted for 12 h under 90 ℃. 1 g TA and 0.5 g oPD were put in ethyl alcohol to heat at 180 ℃ for 12 h. The final products were purified after using silica gel column chromatography, and named G-CNDs, Y-CNDs and R-CNDs separately.

Three types of CNDs can be observed yellow, green and red photoluminescence (PL) respectively (Fig. 2
a-c). In UV region (200–400 nm), the observed absorption bands in UV–vis absorption spectra are the characteristic absorption peaks of aromatic compounds, generally originated from π → π* transitions of C = C/C–C and n → π* transitions of aromatic systems with many C − N/C = N or C − O/C = O structures, respectively [17]. The redshift of the G-CNDs indicates a larger conjugate system, which is consistent with the results of the TEM. The introduction of TA leads to the absorption peak blue shift and intensity increase because of the conjugation between benzene ring and carbonyl. As shown in fluorescence (FL) spectra ( Fig. 2 d-f), three CNDs show green (543 nm), yellow (575 nm) and far-red (609–709 nm) emission. They also exhibit an outstanding excitation-independent FL behavior, indicating the presence of only one emission center. The FL quantum yield (QY) is 3.77% for G-CNDs, 4.74% for Y-CNDs and 13.08% for R-CNDs. Obviously, the QY of R-CNDs has been increased significantly.

The introduction of TA is beneficial to react with oPD to prepare red-emission CNDs by solvothermal method under 180 ℃ for 12 h. Amino group on the benzene of oPD, as a kind of strong electron donor group which has the electron donor hyperconjugate effect, and carboxyl group of TA, as a kind of electron-accepting group with the electronegativity which has an electron-withdrawing tendency, are more likely to react with each other. The carbon core of the R-CNDs is made of sp2-hybridized carbon domain and can be regarded as a great π-conjugated molecule. Therefore, the surface of R-CNDs with many functional groups attached to a carbon core and formed a special donor-π-acceptor (D-π-A) structure, which may cause the redshift of the PL (Fig. 3 g).

In summary, during the reaction of oPD, FeCl3 as a catalyst was helpful for catalytic oxidation polymerization under a low reaction temperature, and PL changed from green to yellow. A kind of D-π-A-conjugated R-CNDs has been successfully prepared by introducing TA, and red FL was obtained, which is a promising application prospect in bio-imaging. More importantly, it is beneficial to provide a reliable avenue for further design of red CNDs.

Yaqing Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Jianliang Bai: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Zhujun Huang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shuang Wu: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xinyu Wang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Guojun Yuan: . Lu Zhang: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lili Ren: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge A Project Funded by the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (1107047002), the Key Research and Development Plan (Modern Agriculture) of Jiangsu Province (BE2018385) and Innovation platform project supported by Jiangsu Province (6907041203).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matlet.2021.131397.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon dots-​Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.Hola, Y.Zhang, Y.Wang, E. P. Giannelis, R. Zboril, A. L. Rogach, Carbon dots-​Emerging light emitters for bioimaging, cancer therapy and optoelectronics, Nano Today 9 (5) (2014) 590-603.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rational Design of Far-Red to Near-Infrared Emitting Carbon Dots for Ultrafast Lysosomal Polarity Imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Sun, H. Qin, X. Geng, R. Yang, L. Qu, A. N. Kani, Z. Li, Rational Design of Far-Red to Near-Infrared Emitting Carbon Dots for Ultrafast Lysosomal Polarity Imaging, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 12 (2020) 31738-31744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based quantum particles: an electroanalytical and biomedical perspective

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Nekoueian, M. Amiri, M. Sillanpaa, F. Marken, R. Boukherroub, S. Szunerits, Carbon-based quantum particles: an electroanalytical and biomedical perspective, Chem. Soc. Rev. 48 (2019) 4281-4316.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Near-infrared chemiluminescent carbon nanodots and their application in reactive oxygen species bioimaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. L. Shen, Q. Lou, J. H. Zang, K. K. Liu, S. N. Qu, L. Dong, C. X. Shan, Near-infrared chemiluminescent carbon nanodots and their application in reactive oxygen species bioimaging, Adv. Sci. 7 (8) (2020) 1903525.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zheng, S. Liu, J. Liu, D. Qu, H. Zhao, X. Guan, X. Hu, Z. Xie, X. Jing, Z. Sun, Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine, Adv. Mater. 26 (2014) 3554-3560.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Liu, Y. Liu, N. Liu, Y. Han, X. Zhang, H. Huang, Y. Lifshitz, S.-T. Lee, J. Zhong, Z. Kang, Metal-free efficient photocatalyst for stable visible water splitting via a two-electron pathway, Science 347 (6225) (2015) 970-974.
P. G. Luo, S. Sahu, S.-T. Yang, S. Sonkar, J. Wang, H. Wang, G. E. LeCroy, L. Cao, Y. Sun, Carbon “Quantum” Dots for Optical Bioimaging. J. Mater. Chem. B 1 (16) (2013) 2116-2127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Xu, T. Kuang, Y. Liu, L. Cai, X. Peng, T. Sreenivasan ; P. Zhao, Z. Yu, N. Li, Heteroatom-doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization, properties, photoluminescence mechanism and biological applications. J. Mater. Chem. B 4 (45) (2016) 7204-7219.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: UV-Vis-NIR Full-Range Responsive Carbon Dots with Large Multiphoton Absorption Cross Sections and Deep-Red Fluorescence at Nucleoli and In Vivo

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Jiang, H. Ding, M. Xu, X. Hu, S. Li, M. Zhang, Q. Zhang, Q. Wang, S. Lu, Y. Tian, H. Bi, UV-Vis-NIR Full-Range Responsive Carbon Dots with Large Multiphoton Absorption Cross Sections and Deep-Red Fluorescence at Nucleoli and In Vivo, Small 16 (19) (2020) 2000680.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon Dots: Surface Engineering and Applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Liu, C. Li, Y. Ren, X. Sun, W. Pan, Y. Li, J. Wang, W. Wang, Carbon Dots: Surface Engineering and Applications, J. Mater. Chem. B (35) (2016) 4 5772-5788.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, S. Sun, L. Zhang, Y. Lu, A. Wu, C. Cai, H. Lin, Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging, Angew. Chem. 127 (18) (2015) 5450 –5453

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of N-doped yellow carbon dots and N, P co-doped red carbon dots for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy of tumors

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Zhao, F. T. Li, S. Zhang, Y. An and S. Q. Sun, Preparation of N-doped yellow carbon dots and N, P co-doped red carbon dots for bioimaging and photodynamic therapy of tumors, New J. Chem. 43 (2019) 6332-6342.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon dots with multiple color emission by controlled graphitization and surface functionalization

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Miao, D. Qu, D. Yang, B. Nie, Y. Zhao, Hong. Fan, Z. Sun, Synthesis of Carbon Dots with Multiple Color Emission by Controlled Graphitization and Surface Functionalization, Adv. Mater. 30 (1) (2018) 1704740

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red, green, and blue light-emitting carbon dots prepared from o-phenylenediamine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. L. An, X. Lin, Y. X. Zhou, Y. Li, Y. W. Zheng, C. H. Wu, K. M. Xu, X. J. Chai and C. Liu, Red, green, and blue light-emitting carbon dots prepared from o-phenylenediamine, RSC Adv. 11 (2021) 26915-26919.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Red dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Li, H. G. Y. Rui, Cheng, J. Z. Guo, Z. B. Liang, G. Li, Q. Li, C. F. Wang, S. Chen, Red dual-emissive carbon dots for ratiometric sensing of veterinary drugs, Journal of Luminescence 236 (2021) 118092.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of multicolor photoluminescent carbon dots by tuning surface states

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Jiang, X. Y. Feng, X. L. Gao, Y. H. Wang, C. Z. Cai, Z. J. Li, H. W. Lin, Preparation of Multicolor Photoluminescent Carbon Dots by Tuning Surface States, Nanomaterials, 9 (4) (2019) 529

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toward high-efficient red emissive carbon dots: facile preparation, unique properties, and applications as multifunctional theranostic agents

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Sun, L. Zhang, K. Jiang, A. Wu, H. Lin, Toward High-Efficient Red Emissive Carbon Dots: Facile Preparation, Unique Properties, and Applications as Multifunctional Theranostic Agents, Chem. Mater. 28 (23) (2016) 8659-8668.
